sentences.text: Yes, I'm in, in Fohrenwald.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was a place where, during the war, there were workers who worked in a hidden rocket factory near there.
It was a underground factory.
So we stayed in those houses where these workers used to work during the war for, for the Germans.
And I didn't realize till later, but we were-- went to that forest.
And under that forest was this huge factory.
And they developed rockets there.
This didn't come till much later.
So we stayed-- I stayed with-- joined up with a, a family-- I mean, with a friend and his mom.
So we, we lived in this one room.
There were just three of us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What were their names?
Sheine was her name-- Endelman.
And, and her son's name is Chaim Endelman.
They-- and she was a wonderful lady.
She was very good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was just like my mom in a way, just a wonderful lady.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My friend, Chaim, was about a year older.
After being there two years together, he went to Israel.
They actually-- they almost-- they recruited him, the Haganah, at that time-- the Defense Army of-- before this-- Israel became a state.
They came to these displaced-person camps.
And they looked for young people.
Or they volunteered, but they were willingly to volunteer to come to Israel.
And so he left.
And, and his mom and I stayed together.
Yes.
There was a DP camp.
And there was just food-- I mean, you know, was by the UNRRA organization-- the UNRRA, I guess.
And also there was-- the, the, the Joint Distribution Center helped.
The-- there was another organization that helped with schooling.
So I had some informal school.
There was also trade school they had there.
I was there for almost three years.
Well, two and a half, something.
I came in '45, I think-- in the fall of '45 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or late summer.
I don't remember exactly-- late summer, maybe August.
And I was there till-- till April-- March, 1948.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Meanwhile, my aunt and uncle found out that I'm there, from the United States.
So--
Through someone who met me in Rowne.
And he got to the United States before, you know.
And he got there in 1947, '46.
And he knew-- he saw me.
He saw me.
He, he told my aunt and uncle that I'm doing wheeling and dealing-- it was like, you know, businessman, 14-year-old.
So, when they found out I was in that camp, I remember I had a phone call in 1947.
You know, that's like going to the moon, in those days, you know, a telephone call all the way from the United States.
So-- and they-- they wanted me to register for the United States.
I actually wanted to go to Israel.
I waited for the-- to go to Israel.
At that time, they had mostly illegal migration.
They were sneaking people-- Jews-- in to Israel.
But I waited so long.
They kept on telling me, why don't you wait until it's-- they legalize it?
Because all these ships were illegal.
And, being by myself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they discouraged me from going, the, the organization.
They normally-- and, and there are a lot of kids who did go in there, go to Israel.
But most of them were, like, kibbutzim-organized groups.
And I was not part of that.
Well, finally, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, I had notice to-- if I want to go to Israel-- to the United States-- papers arrived.
I decided to go to the United States.
DP camp was just like a little town-- brick buildings.
Streets-- they renamed the streets.
It was all American names-- American states-- Ohio and, and Kentucky-- all, all American.
I guess-- I don't know whether they-- there were soldiers in the beginning there, American soldiers.
May have been at-- yes, American soldiers were in the beginning, when they occupied that part, you know, there were American troops.
And then they left.
And all the streets had American names.
So-- and they had a huge-- a public bath.
We could go take a bath, you know, like, a couple times a week.
And-- pardon?
Oh, that was fantastic, yeah.
I couldn't-- yeah.
I remember-- strange, because they have-- there was still a few Americans there.
And they were leaving.
And I remember when I came in, and there was some-- a bar of soap in the shower.
And it smelled so good.
That soap, I mean, was just something I hadn't seen in years.
Actually, as a kid I'd never seen-- I mean, I never had a soap that smells good.
We used to have soap.
But it didn't smell good at all.
But-- so I do remember about that.
It was just wonderful to take a-- to take a shower-- to stand in a shower.
As a child, we never, never had a shower.
We'd taken-- you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hot-water baths, but not, uh-- so that was great.
The food was, was fine.
I mean, you know, it was-- you know, as a kid, I guess, there was never enough.
But it was OK.
And there was some schooling.
So we lived, you know, compared to what I had, it was pretty decent.
And I cut out that business.
I didn't want to do anymore of that.
In fact, I probably could have made a lot of money because there was some opportunity.
But I didn't want a part of it.
I had given all that up.
There was a O-R-T school, ORT.
You heard of that?
That's an organization that they normally support the school, informal schooling-- or it was mechanical school.
We did some, I remember, mechanical drawings.
And we worked-- we had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] worked on lathes and all kinds of stuff.
We had a-- a engineer came from-- a Russian Jew that used to teach us electrical motors.
And then we had this guy who was a, a, a lawyer but a lover of history.
So he used to teach us ancient history.
So, you know, all the kind of interesting people.
They were all survivors.
It was survivors in that-- in that camp.
There was-- actually, later on, they had more of a, a formal school.
But somehow, I got into this trade school and all that, so I didn't go to that.
And they were teaching Hebrew, also.
So I was there for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] close to three years.
Well, there was-- yeah.
At that time, there, I really had no fear.
I had no fear.
No, I got used to it very quickly, overnight, you know, that-- now, I do remember, when I was in Fohrenwald, there's a group of people came to see me.
And they talked about-- for me to tell them my story.
Because I was one of the few that was just alone, didn't have anybody, didn't have a brother or sister or just-- with me.
I was by myself.
Most of them were a part of a group, part of a family.
I was by myself.
So they, they wondered, hey, this guy, you know, how did he do it?
And, and they came to, to interview me.
And they were some historical committee.
And I didn't-- A German committee?
No, no, this was also-- it was, like, a little government in that camp, Jewish government.
And they did all kinds of things.
So they, they took charge right away.
There was a little government.

And there was, uh-- also, part of the Joint Distribution was some representative from the United States and, you know, things like that, you know, offices where you could register to go to different countries.
And there were always somebody to help you do things.
It's like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] social workers in here, you know.
So, when they came, I was not interested.
I didn't want to tell them anything.
I thought, what-- what's, what's the point?
I mean, you know.
I mean, all these people are survivors.
And they had concentration camps.
But I just didn't want to talk about it.
And that was the main thing.
I just didn't want to talk about it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
It's just too bad that I-- I mean, who thought about diary in those days?
I mean, you couldn't-- when we were crossing the borders, I had a picture of my brother when I found out he was alive.
And he sent me a picture.
They told me to tear it up.
You don't need-- you know, you have no contact with anybody.
You were a survivor in a camp.
And you're going to Greece.
And they search you.
I mean, some silly things.
So I, I had to discard my brother's pictures.
I mean, these organizations, while we were crossing borders from one, you know, country to another.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing with me, even my-- the piece of paper that I got from the post office with my name on it, I had a Russian-- my first name was-- I-- now, if I said 'Isaac,' it would be Jewish.
So I had 'Alexander,' just like my brother Sergei, and my other brother was Misha.
So I also had a-- like, a Russian first name.
[SIGH]
Was Sergei, yes.
And Abraham was Misha in Russia, so-- Mikhail.
So they, you know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, I didn't.
I didn't.
They came-- yeah.
I, I guess they had all the-- yeah, so-- so I was ready to leave for the United States.
And actually, the lady that I stayed with, her son was-- went to Israel.
And she, too, already left for Israel.
So I was basically by myself.
So I was happy to leave.
And I came to New York.
I also had contact with the aunt of mine that lived in Chicago.
And I was sort of debating whether to go to Chicago or to Columbus.
That was my father's sister that I've never known.
And I've never seen her.
And she had two sons.
And they lived in Chicago.
And I-- when I started corresponding with them to that camp, I found-- I found out their addresses to, I think, to somebody-- through my aunt in the United States.
She told me about that.
And I start corresponding with them.
So they, too, asked me to come to Chicago.
My aunt's name was-- Wallach was the name-- Wallace, actually.
It was Wallace.
And she-- I forgot her first name.
She was an elderly person.
But my cousins-- my first two cousins, Morris and Isaac, they were-- they, they-- my first cousins had sons.
One of their sons was older than I am.
So, you see, you could see my aunt was her-- about 80 years old when I saw her.
She was an elderly woman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but I got to-- like, my cousins, they were nice people.
And they wanted me to come to stay with them.
And I was debating where to go.
I decided to go to Columbus.
Well, I remembered my aunt.
And I remembered my uncle, I mean, the guy she married.
And I had more family there.
So, I mean, it was just a tough decision for, for me to make.
At that time, I had no contact with them at all.
I didn't have their, you know, their addresses or anything.
And I actually-- when I waited in Germany all these years, three years, a lot of Russian people, Russian Jews, came from Russia into Germany.
And soon, I heard that a group came.
I went to that camp to see.
I, I travelled all over Germany.
And, and, and, and I saw these tent camps.
They would set up camps, tent camps.
And they had the list of all the people.
And I went through the list, looking for my brothers.
But I hoped they would be there.
And then I finally have given up because they just were not coming.
So I had no contact with them at all.
Yes, they knew everything about that.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
No, I told them right away.
I mean, they know that-- I told them I'm the only one that survived, you know.
They knew that.
And they probably-- I told them the parents are not living, yeah, maybe didn't go into detail.
So, I mean, they, they, they didn't expect anybody to be alive.
And here I am.
So they knew the rest of it without me telling them.
But I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] so-- came to Columbus.
But you arrived in New York.
Oh, I, I arrived in New York.
I was there.
There was an area where a lot of refugees came before they went other places.
Pardon?
Yeah.
There was a-- yeah, we went on one of the old Army-- Navy ships.
It was a very crowded boat.
I got seasick.
Everybody else did, too.
It took us about two weeks to cross the ocean.
We had these unusual waves and storms on the sea.
It was very tough traveling on a ship and being sick.
But I was happy that I made it to New York.
It was just-- at, at that time, I was just wondering what life is going to be like.
I mean, that was another thing.
There was-- I had more fear of all these things-- what's going to happen?
What were you afraid of?
Well, afraid of-- you know, here, in a country where-- language barrier-- I couldn't speak the language.
And what am I going to do?
How am I going to do?
How they-- my relatives going to be towards me, and all that.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I came.
And I came to Columbus.
Yes.
No-- from my family?
No, they didn't know exactly when I'm going to arrive or anything like that.
But there, there were people-- organizational people, social workers-- who were involved.
In fact, I remember when I was in that old hotel, there was a young man came.
And he took me to see a movie.
He took me out to dinner.
And he was very nice.
Then he took me to the train when I left New York, got me a little gift.
And I could never figure out who he was, whether a volunteer or part of the organization.
He spoke a little Yiddish, very little.
So we were able to communicate that way.
[LAUGHS] No.
But I do, do remember the commercials that were running-- for president.
There was, you know, Truman and Wallace, vice president.
I remember seeing that.
But I don't remember the movie.
Yes.
Right.
It was in English.
I was in New York for about five, six days, I think.
I stayed-- it's an old hotel, I think, where all these refugees came in to stay there.
Not all of them-- maybe one of the places, I think.
It was just-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yes, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took the train-- this gentleman took me to the train.
While I was on the train, again, I, I was thinking, how am I going to get to-- how-- even to my family's house?
I mean, I didn't know-- because I couldn't communicate at all.
So I walked in the train.
And there were not too many people in that train at night.
And I-- well, it was early in the morning.
And I saw this guy, this old fellow, with a Jewish newspaper, reading.
Yeah, I couldn't see his face, but I saw the newspaper.
So I started talking to him in, in Yiddish.
And he told me that he's coming to Columbus.
And he says-- he wrote down a thing how-- what to say to the cab driver.
So-- and, and to give the cab driver my address.
So I did just that.
And I get to the house.
And, as I understand, my aunt and uncle came to the train station to meet me.
But they missed me.
[LAUGHS] They just-- I don't know how, but they missed me.
And I missed them.
So here I am, at my aunt and uncle's house, which was in Columbus on 22nd Avenue.
And I have three little cousins, young cousins.
The oldest, Cookie, was about eight years old, and with a babysitter.
And here we can't communicate.
You know, we can't talk.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I found out, later on, my aunt and uncle came back.
And they said, you know, where were you?
[LAUGHS] You know, like, we were looking for you!
So-- So the babysitter let you in the house, and you waited for your aunt and uncle?
Yeah, they let-- oh, sure, everybody waited.
The kids were excited.
You know, they were waiting for their cousin to come and all that.
So they were all excited.
But here I am, like an idiot, you know-- couldn't communicate.
That was a very tough part.
But fortunately my aunt, you know, we spoke Yiddish-- and my, my uncle.
And--
Yeah.
My aunt's name was Sheine. 'Sheine' means 'pretty'-- and, and, and, and Jack Gelin.
That was my uncle's name, Jack.
And I remembered him, too.
I mean, he knew me most of my life.
And then I had other uncles.
And I also started meetings cousins and couldn't get them straight for quite a while, didn't know who was who.
Because I did have a pretty large family here.
Yeah, we didn't talk very much, just little bits and pieces come out.
Well, they knew they were dead there.
But I don't know if anybody wanted real details.
I mean, it was tough for them to, to swallow and for me to talk.
So there was-- not a great deal was said.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we talked a little bit at a time.
But I, I remember-- oh, that was later on, when I was in school-- I started the-- they put me in the ninth grade.
I was 18 years old.
I don't know how they chose the ninth grade-- but not being able to speak at all.
In fact, my cousin [SIGHS] took me to Central High-- Central High School in the summertime.
And she was going to help me register.
And we walk up the steps.
And I turn around, and I'm by myself.
My cousin is not even there.
And I look, and I don't know what happened.
So I walked in the school.
And I didn't know how to go about it.
I didn't know what to say to anybody.
Somehow, I managed.
I registered.
And I, I chose two subjects-- which, of course, English.
And then I thought, well, maybe Algebra-- Algebra, maybe.
I don't know why.
Not that I thought I was going to be great at it, maybe.
But I had an awful time-- six weeks.
Because I couldn't understand, couldn't communicate.
They passed me with two Ds.
This was summer school.
My cousin disappeared.
She was not there, so I was all by myself.
You know, you won't believe that-- till, till three years ago-- three years ago, I said, Leah, do you remember, you took me to the school, and you dropped me off?
And you were gone.
She doesn't remember that.
She's said for years she didn't remember that.
And I thought, maybe she did it purposely.
Hey, this is how-- a good way of-- you know, this is what I thought all these years.
But two years ago, she said, you know, I, I, I thought what happened.
She says, I parked in the wrong place.
And they told me to move the car before I get a-- they remove it.
She says, so, by time I went in the car and I drove around and-- it was-- she didn't see me.
So that's how it came about.
[LAUGHS]
My--?
Yeah, I think I just-- I didn't know whether I was happy or not happy.
Because, I mean, you know, I already had been to the United States.
I mean, I'd been-- already had freedom for awhile.
But, but I think I looked for the-- you know, I just didn't know what to expect-- how's things going to be.
My aunt was a pretty strict person at one time.
And I remembered as a kid-- and always bothered me when she was visiting us.
I was six years old.
She used to yell at us when we were just young kids.
Well, I was the youngest, and, and next to my-- and then my, my sister.
So I wondered about that.
What's it going to be like, to-- living with, you know, three little cousins and my aunt and uncle and all that?
So-- What were your cousins' names?
My cousin's name-- in fact, there's a picture right on top.
There's-- Cookie is-- she was the oldest.
And then Benson.
And he was also named after my grandfather, like my little brother.
And Eddie--
Yeah, Benzion, that's-- and Eddie.
Eddie was two years old.
Benson was, like, five.
And Cookie was around eight.
Leah was a cousin of mine that-- she was an older cousin.
She's still alive.
She's 83 years old right now.
She was my, my mother's-- her mother and my mother were sisters, yeah.
Yeah.
